turns.content: I'm so tired of my CI failing on missing arguments. It's like Python where you get a TypeError if I don't pass the right keyword, but here in Rust it just refuses to compile and I have no idea why. Can you explain how this is similar to that scripting pain?

That frustration comes from losing the runtime hint of Python's optional parameters. In Rust, you want to enforce those constraints at compile time so the build never fails because a user forgot a field. A full proc macro can generate a builder pattern that makes every required field mandatory until explicitly set.
I think I understand the idea but not how it actually works under the hood. Is this just like implementing a trait manually or something more magical?

It is neither manual nor purely magical; it's a full proc macro that runs during compilation to expand your derive attribute into actual code. You define the builder logic once in the macro, then every struct using #[derive(MyBuilder)] gets transformed into a fluent API with compile-time checks.
Okay so I'm writing this macro? Does it need to implement any specific traits or interact with serde_json::Value?

You will likely use syn crate to parse the input structs and quote crate to emit the builder code. You do not need serde_json::Value directly unless you want to generate serialization helpers, but the core logic stays within syn's AST representation.
This sounds complicated for my first Rust project. Can I just use existing crates instead of writing a custom derive?

There are crates like builder-pattern or similar that provide basic builders, but they lack the compile-time enforcement you need. A full proc macro gives you exact control over which fields must be present and how errors surface.
I still don't get why this matters for my CI pipeline. Isn't just adding a validation check enough?

A runtime check in your code will fail the build only if you run that specific function; but with our proc macro, any missing required field becomes an immediate compilation error, preventing broken configs from ever reaching CI.
Wait a minute. If I just add a validation method in my struct, why can't I do that instead of this whole macro thing?

Because you need the builder pattern itself to enforce the order and presence of fields automatically during construction. A simple validation method does not prevent users from skipping steps; our proc macro generates a builder where omitting required fields is literally impossible at compile time.
I see now, but what happens if I make an optional field? How do the macros know which ones are required?

The macro scans your struct definition and marks specific fields as required while leaving others optional. When a user tries to build without a required field, the generated code produces a compile-time error instead of silently accepting an incomplete config.

I've been wrestling with Rust's builder pattern in Java. I tried to use serde_json::Value and impl Serialize, but I need a compile-time error if fields are missing. I wrote a custom derive macro that generates a builder struct with required/optional fields using syn and quote crates. It works for basic types like String and i32, but fails when the user forgets to set an optional field at runtime.

The issue is that your macro generates logic to validate presence, but if you're checking fields via Option<T> or nullable types without explicit defaults, the compiler won't catch missing values at compile time. You need to ensure every field in your builder has a default value of None or 0 so the generated code can enforce presence checks. The derive macro should generate a Builder struct where each optional field is wrapped in an Option type with a default of None.

That makes sense, but my generated builder still allows incomplete objects to pass through. I'm using serde_derive for the final serialization, so maybe I should just rely on that? Or is there something wrong with how syn parses optional fields?

Relying solely on serde_derive won't work because it doesn't enforce runtime presence checks for optional fields. Your custom derive must generate a Builder struct where every field is explicitly typed as Option<T> with a default of None, and then you add a method like has_field() that returns true only if the value was set. This ensures compile-time safety via type system while allowing runtime validation.

I tried adding a method to check if all required fields are present, but my macro isn't generating it. Is syn parsing optional attributes correctly? Maybe I need to use derive macro instead of proc-macro crate?

You're using the right approach with a full proc-macro, not a simple derive attribute. The problem is likely that your syn parsing logic isn't handling optional attributes properly. You need to explicitly check for attributes like #[serde(skip)] or #[serde(default = "default_fn")] in your macro implementation. If you don't parse these correctly, the generated Builder won't include the necessary validation methods.
I added attribute parsing to my syn code, but now I'm getting errors from quote crate when generating the builder struct. Should I use a different macro generator or is there a simpler way?

The error likely comes from incorrect syntax in your quote::quote! macro expansion. You need to ensure all generated code uses proper Rust syntax with correct semicolons and braces. Instead of debugging quote errors, try simplifying the output first by printing the generated struct directly using eprintln! to verify it compiles before integrating into larger code.
After fixing the syntax, I realized my builder doesn't have a way to set optional fields. Should I add a default constructor or use a separate method for each field?

Add a default constructor that initializes all fields with None/0 values, then provide individual setter methods like set_name() and set_age(). This gives you flexibility to build objects partially while ensuring the Builder struct enforces presence checks via runtime validation.
I added setters but now I need to validate that all required fields are present before building. Is there a trait I can implement for this, or should I just add a build() method?

Implement a build() method on your Builder struct that returns the final object only if all required fields have been set. This acts as a compile-time and runtime guard against incomplete objects. You can also add an Option<Error> return type to indicate which fields were missing, but a simple bool or Result<T> is often cleaner.
I added the build() method but it returns a Result instead of just T. Should I use std::result::Result for error handling? Or is there a better pattern?

Use std::result::Result<T, BuilderError> where BuilderError contains details about missing fields. This gives you explicit control over error messages and avoids generic panic behavior. If you want simplicity, just return Option<T> with None indicating incomplete objects.
I implemented Result<T, BuilderError>, but now I need to serialize the final object. My serde_derive isn't handling optional fields correctly in the output JSON.

The serialization issue is likely due to how your generated struct defines optional fields. Ensure every field is wrapped in Option<T> and use #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")] on each one so only set values appear in JSON output.
I added the skip_serializing_if attribute, but now I'm getting warnings about unused imports in my syn crate. Should I clean up the macro or is this normal?

Unused import warnings are common when you're testing partial functionality. Clean them up by removing unnecessary imports from your macro's use statement, but don't worry if some are temporary until the full implementation is complete.
I cleaned up the imports and now my custom derive compiles without errors. But I'm still getting runtime panics when building incomplete objects. Is there a way to catch these at compile time?

You can't fully prevent runtime panics for missing optional fields, but you can add a compile-time check by using Rust's type system. If you define required fields as non-Option types and optional fields as Option<T>, the compiler will ensure required fields are always present at compile time.
I was thinking of this like the builder pattern I used in Java, but I want to make sure it works with Rust's strict rules.
That's a great comparison. In Rust we achieve this using derive macros and traits instead of manual boilerplate, which keeps the code cleaner and safer at compile time.
So I don't have to write any logic for the builder manually? Can a macro do all that?
Exactly. A custom derive macro can analyze your struct definition and automatically generate the builder implementation, handling field validation and ordering.
What kind of attributes do I need to add to my struct for this to work?
You would use the derive macro on your struct, and inside the macro you can define required fields that must be present at compile time or optional ones with defaults.
Does this guarantee I get a runtime error if I forget a field?
Yes, the macro ensures that missing required fields cause a compile-time error, so you never accidentally create an incomplete builder at runtime.
I'm worried about making my code too complex. Is there a simpler way?
For advanced use cases, a full proc macro is the best balance of automation and control, but for simple structs you might just use standard builder patterns without macros.
What if I want to add validation logic like checking that a field is not empty?
You can extend the macro to call traits or functions from crates like serde_json or std::vec for additional checks before returning the builder.
Can I use this with existing libraries too?
Yes, as long as your struct implements traits like Serialize or Clone that the macro expects, you can integrate it seamlessly.
I want to know if I need to worry about performance with this approach?
No, the macro runs at compile time so it doesn't impact runtime performance, and the generated builder is as fast as manually written one.
What about error handling when I try to build something invalid?
The macro will produce clear errors if you violate field requirements, which is better than runtime panics for catching mistakes early.
Could I combine this with other macros like serde to simplify my workflow?
Absolutely, you can stack multiple derive calls so your struct gets both serialization and builder capabilities without extra code.
One last thing: what happens if I change the field names after deriving?
You would need to re-derive or update your struct, but once generated, the builder won't automatically adapt unless you use a dynamic macro system.

I'm trying to implement a custom derive macro in Rust that generates a builder pattern. I thought I could just use the standard derive macros like serde_derive or clap_derive, but they don't seem to support compile-time validation for missing fields. Is there a way to make this work without writing a full proc-macro crate?

Actually, standard derive macros like serde_derive or clap_derive can generate builders, but they don't enforce compile-time errors for missing fields by default. To get that behavior you need to write a full proc-macro crate using the proc_macro crate and implement your own CustomDerive trait. The standard library doesn't provide this functionality out of the box.
Okay, so I'll need to create a separate proc-macro crate. That sounds complicated. Can I just extend an existing macro like serde_derive or derive_more instead?

You can't really extend macros like serde_derive or derive_more directly for this purpose because they're already full proc-macro crates with their own logic. What you need is a completely separate crate that implements your CustomDerive trait and generates the builder code at compile time. The derive_more crate might be useful later, but right now you need to build your own macro system from scratch.
I see. So I'll write a new crate with a CustomDerive trait and use that in my code? What should the output look like when I apply this derive?

When you apply your custom derive to a struct, it will generate a builder pattern at compile time. The generated code will have required fields marked as required and optional fields marked as optional, with compile-time errors if any required fields are missing when constructing the object.
That sounds exactly like what I need! But how do I actually implement the CustomDerive trait? Do I need to parse the AST or something?

You'll need to parse the AST using syn crate and handle errors with Result types. The macro will analyze your struct definition, identify required vs optional fields, then generate the builder code using quote! macro from proc-macro2. You can't avoid parsing the syntax tree entirely.
So I'll use syn crate to parse the input, then use quote! to generate the builder implementation? What about error handling if someone forgets a required field?

Yes, you'll use syn crate to parse the input AST and quote! from proc-macro2 to generate the builder code. For errors, you can either emit compile-time diagnostics through the macro's error handling or provide runtime validation with a custom trait implementation that checks field presence.
I think I understand now. But what if someone tries to use this on a struct with default values? Will it still generate compile-time errors?

If you define required fields in your macro configuration, they will always trigger compile-time errors even with default values. The proc-macro can detect whether a field has a default implementation and adjust its treatment accordingly, but the core behavior remains: missing required fields cause compilation failures.
Great! One last question - should I use serde_json or some other serialization crate with this builder system?

You can integrate serde_json or any other serialization crate alongside your custom derive. The builder pattern you're creating works independently of serialization, so it will complement crates like serde_json without conflicting with their existing implementations.

I'm trying to migrate an old C++ project to Rust and I thought I could just use the standard derive macros to automatically generate a builder pattern with required fields. I saw the Derive trait in documentation so I assumed there was a single macro that would handle everything.

That's a common misconception for new Rust developers. You cannot use standard derive macros alone to generate a full builder pattern with compile-time enforcement of required fields. Those features require a custom proc-macro crate like mycros or similar tools that analyze the struct definition and emit code at compile time.

Oh, I see. So I need to write a full proc macro myself? That sounds tedious compared to what I thought it was. I thought there might be something like serde that could do this automatically without any extra code in my source files.

Serde does not generate builders; it only handles serialization and deserialization. To get a builder with required fields, you need to implement the trait yourself or use a dedicated crate that provides this logic. You cannot rely on standard library macros alone for this specific pattern.

I understand now. So I should write my own proc macro using the quote and syn crates? That sounds like what I need to do. But wait, if I just add a derive attribute to my struct, won't that automatically trigger the builder generation?

If you write your own proc macro, yes, adding a custom derive attribute like #[mycros_builder] will trigger it. However, standard Rust macros do not work this way out of the box unless you define one first. You cannot just assume any struct with a derive attribute will get a builder without defining that specific macro logic.

Right, so I need to create a custom proc macro crate and then use it in my project. But what if I want to support optional fields too? Can the same macro handle both required and optional without writing separate logic for each?

Yes, a well-designed proc macro can analyze your struct definition and generate different builder methods based on field attributes. You might use derive macros to parse the struct fields and emit either required or optional builder methods in the output code.

That makes sense. So the macro looks at my struct, sees which fields are required versus optional, and then generates a Builder type with different constructors for each case. But I'm worried about performance since this is running at compile time.

Proc-macro crates run at compile time in the cargo build process, so they do not impact runtime performance of your application. They only affect compilation speed slightly, which is generally acceptable for most projects.
Good to know about that performance distinction. So if I'm building a tool where compile time matters more than runtime, should I avoid using complex proc macros? Or is it fine?

For most cases involving builder patterns, the complexity of your proc macro does not significantly impact build times. However, if you have extremely large codebases or tight deadlines, simpler approaches might be preferable.
I see. But what about error messages? If I forget to provide a required field at runtime, how does the builder tell me what's missing?

The proc macro generates code that enforces this at compile time. If you try to build an object without providing all required fields, the compiler will reject it with a clear error message pointing to the exact field that's missing.

That's much better than runtime checks which can be confusing. But I'm still concerned about maintaining this custom macro across my team. Is there documentation or examples available for common patterns?

There are several crates like mycros that provide good documentation and examples of how to implement these patterns. They show you how to parse struct definitions, handle different field types, and generate the appropriate builder code.

Perfect. So my path forward is to start with one of those crates, study their examples, then adapt it for my specific project needs. And I'll need to make sure all my team members understand this new pattern once we adopt it.

Exactly. Start by cloning a working implementation from an existing crate like mycros, then modify it to match your project's specific field types and requirements. Once the macro is set up, document the usage patterns for your team so everyone knows how to use the builder correctly.
